title: What is DETR?
concept: DETR uses a Transformer encoder-decoder to predict a direct set of bounding boxes, removing manual anchor and NMS components.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main benefit of Feature Pyramid Networks (FPNs)?

options.text: They merge low-level spatial details with high-level semantic context to improve multi-scale detection
They convert the model into a classification-only network
They remove the need for a CNN backbone

exercises.explain: Correct! FPNs create a pyramid of feature maps of different resolutions to capture objects of all sizes.

cards.title: Throwing Away the Rules
cards.body: For years, object detection was dominated by CNNs, anchor boxes, grids, and NMS cleanup. In 2020, researchers asked a crazy question: What if we threw all of those manual tricks away and used a Transformer instead? enter DETR.

cards.title: What is DETR?
cards.body: DETR (DEtection TRansformer) is an object detection model that uses a Transformer encoder-decoder architecture. It treats object detection as a direct set prediction problem, completely removing manual components like anchor boxes and Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS).

cards.title: The Executive Assistant
cards.body: Traditional detectors are like a manager drawing a grid and having 100 workers check and cleanup overlapping boxes. DETR is like hiring an executive assistant: they look at the scene once, identify all objects, and list them directly (e.g. '1. Car, 2. Dog, 3. Empty').

cards.title: Direct Set Output
cards.body: When given a street image, DETR directly outputs a fixed set of 100 predictions. If the image has only 2 cars, the model matches the 2 cars to the best 2 predictions and sets the remaining 98 predictions to the class 'no object'.

cards.title: Backbone Myth
cards.body: You might think DETR does not use a CNN at all since it is a Transformer. Fact: DETR still uses a standard CNN backbone (like ResNet) to extract raw feature maps first, which are then flattened into tokens for the Transformer.

cards.title: Traditional vs. DETR Pipeline
cards.body: How the detection workflow changes with Transformers:

left.text: CNN -> Grid/Anchors -> Hundreds of raw predictions -> NMS cleanup -> Final boxes

right.text: CNN -> Transformer Encoder/Decoder -> Direct set of 100 predictions -> 1-to-1 matching -> Final boxes

exercises.prompt: What is the core conceptual change introduced by DETR?

options.text: Treating object detection as a direct set prediction problem using a Transformer
Using a recursive CNN to upscale images
Classifying the image color palette first

exercises.explain: Correct! DETR uses a Transformer to predict a direct set of bounding boxes without anchors or NMS.

exercises.prompt: True or False: DETR completely eliminates the need for Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) during post-processing.
exercises.explain: True. Because DETR is trained to predict exactly one box per object, it has no duplicate boxes and skips NMS entirely.

exercises.prompt: Why does DETR still require a CNN backbone like ResNet?

options.text: To extract initial 2D feature maps from the raw image pixels
To perform the final classification step
To run the Hungarian matching algorithm

exercises.explain: Correct! CNNs are highly efficient at extracting low-level 2D features, which are then passed to the Transformer.

exercises.prompt: You are building a real-time system where any latency spikes are dangerous. Why is DETR's NMS-free design attractive for this application?

options.text: Because NMS speed depends on the number of predicted boxes, which varies. DETR has a fixed prediction pipeline, giving highly predictable latency.
Transformers do not use mathematical calculations.
DETR does not use GPU memory.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Eliminating NMS removes a variable-time post-processing bottleneck, which stabilizes inference latency.
